headline_suffix: builds roughly 15% more of it than once — at the same weekly volume.
why_it_matters: Your upper/lower 4-day split is built on exactly this. Don't try to cram all your chest work into Upper A and skim Upper B — the same total sets, split across two days, grow you measurably faster for free.

headline_suffix: per muscle per week — less is leaving gains on the table, more is just fatigue.
citation: Schoenfeld et al. (2018) — dose-response volume meta-analysis
why_it_matters: Count your weekly sets per muscle. If chest is sitting at 8, add a couple of sets to your Upper B day. If it's already 22, you're not growing more — you're just paying a recovery tax that bleeds into your next lower session.

headline_suffix: grows the same muscle as grinding to zero — with a fraction of the recovery cost.
why_it_matters: If today's voice memo says 'felt fried', you don't need to push every set into the redline. Leave 1–2 in the tank on your compounds, save failure for the last set of curls or lateral raises, and you'll show up fresher on Friday's lower day.

headline_prefix: Light and heavy weights build muscle
headline_suffix: — as long as both sets are taken near failure.
citation: Morton et al. / Schoenfeld load-equating studies (golden-encyklopedia-building-muscle.md)
why_it_matters: On a day your shoulder or knee is whispering, you don't have to PR. Drop the bar 20% and chase 12 hard reps — the growth is the same. Your joints will thank you next session.

headline_suffix: once you hit the top of your rep range — that's the entire engine of growth.
why_it_matters: Pick 6–10 reps. Start at 6, add a rep each session, jump the weight 5% the week you nail 10 across all sets, drop back to 6. Apply this to your bench, row, squat and RDL — it's the simplest way to make sure your plan below isn't the same one you ran last month.

headline_suffix: — anything less and your next set is a cardio set, not a strength set.
why_it_matters: On your squat and bench in the plan below, don't rush. Wait until your breathing settles. Sixty-second rests feel productive but they cut your working weight by 10–15%, which is exactly the volume-at-intensity you came for.

headline_suffix: — 50–60% volume at the same loads — is what keeps joints, tendons and CNS in the game.
why_it_matters: Deloads aren't weakness, they're how your tendons catch up to your muscles. If you've been pushing the plan below for 5–6 weeks straight and weights feel sticky, this week's lighter sets aren't a setback — they're the reason next block PRs.

headline_prefix: A single bout of isometric holds can drop tendon pain by
headline_suffix: — five sets of 45 seconds at 70% effort.
why_it_matters: If an elbow or shoulder tendon is barking during warm-ups, do five 45-second holds at a pain-free angle before you lift. You'll get a 45-minute window where pain signaling is quieted enough to train cleanly — and you're rebuilding the tendon in the process.

highlight: dips, behind-the-neck presses and deep flyes
headline_suffix: — all three load the anterior capsule in exactly the overextended position that hurt you.
why_it_matters: If your shoulder is even whispering this week, sub close-grip bench for dips, a landmine press for any overhead barbell work, and a pec deck with limited stretch for flyes. The plan below already biases this way — don't override it with 'I felt fine warming up'.

headline_suffix: produces more hypertrophy and stronger tendon adaptation than short-range work.
why_it_matters: On RDLs, get the bar to mid-shin. On incline curls, let the elbow drift back. On split squats, sink the back knee. The stretched portion of each rep is where the growth actually lives — half-reps build half a muscle.

headline_prefix: Before working sets, ramp the bar at
headline_suffix: — five short sets that cost nothing and protect the next 20 minutes of heavy work.
why_it_matters: If you're walking into bench at 80 kg, that's bar × 10, 40 × 5, 55 × 3, 70 × 1, then go. It takes four minutes and it's the difference between a clean working set and tweaking something that costs you a week.

headline_suffix: beats the workout you'd do on six — every single time.
why_it_matters: Deep sleep is when growth hormone peaks, motor patterns lock in, and your nervous system resets. A skipped session at 8 hours of sleep recovers faster than a forced one at 5. If today's memo says 'wrecked, slept badly', that's a real input — push the heavy day a slot.

headline_suffix: — beating ice baths, stretching, stim and compression in head-to-head trials.
why_it_matters: Ten to twenty minutes with a foam roller or gun on quads, hams, lats and pecs after your hardest sessions is the highest-ROI recovery tool available to you. Skip the ice bath unless inflammation is the actual problem — it can blunt the gains you just earned.

headline_suffix: — save them for 4+ hours after lifting, or for rest days.
why_it_matters: The inflammation right after a hard set is the signal your body uses to rebuild. Plunging straight after the gym dampens that signal. If you love the cold, take it on Sunday or hours after your session — not as a finisher.

headline_suffix: can blunt muscle and strength gains via mTOR interference — keep them at least 6 hours apart.
why_it_matters: Keep lifting and hard running on separate days when you can. If you ever stack a run before a lift, you're paying interest on every set that follows. Walking is fine; hard running before squats is not.

headline_prefix: Protein benefits for muscle plateau at
headline_suffix: — past that, you're not growing more, just paying more for groceries.
why_it_matters: Past ~1.6 g/kg, extra protein doesn't add muscle — it just adds expense. Hit the floor every day; stop chasing 250 g.

headline_prefix: In a caloric deficit, raise protein to
headline_suffix: — that's the dose that shields muscle while fat falls.
why_it_matters: If you're cutting, the bump to ~2.4 g/kg on training days is the difference between recomp (muscle up, fat down) and just losing weight. Maintenance-day protein can sit a touch lower.

highlight: 6 to 10 hard sets per muscle per week
headline_suffix: is enough to grow — anything less and you're maintaining, not building.
why_it_matters: Travel week, sick week, busy week — if you can hit just 6 quality sets per muscle, you're not regressing. Don't skip the gym thinking 'it's not enough to matter'. Two sets of three exercises is plenty to hold the line.

headline_suffix: — heavy enough for tension, light enough to recover.
why_it_matters: This is where your compound work in the plan below should live. Triples are for strength meets, sets of 20 are for finishers. The big middle is where you'll get the most muscle for the least joint cost.

highlight: wall slides, external rotations and Y/T/W holds
headline_suffix: before pressing is the cheapest shoulder insurance you can buy.
why_it_matters: Given your shoulder history, this is non-negotiable before any upper-body day. Wall slides × 10, banded external rotations × 12 each, face pulls × 15, one round of Y/T/W. Then bench. Skip it once and you'll feel it on rep 3 of set 2.

highlight: the last set of an isolation lift
headline_suffix: — never grind it on bench, squat or deadlift.
why_it_matters: Push the final set of curls, lateral raises and leg extensions into the red. Leave your heavy compounds at 1–2 RIR. Grinding a max-effort bench rep costs you three days of bench progress; grinding a curl costs you nothing.

headline_suffix: — 60–70% of weekly volume should come from squat, deadlift, bench, row and press patterns.
why_it_matters: Open every session with the lift that demands the most. The plan below puts squats and bench up front for exactly this reason. Don't burn out on cable work and then half-rep your main lift — the order on the page is the order to lift in.

headline_suffix: isn't a badge — it's a signal you're under-recovering.
why_it_matters: If you're still walking sore on Thursday from Monday's squats, the answer isn't more protein shakes — it's a deload week or a 200–300 calorie bump for 1–2 weeks. Chronic soreness is volume your body can't pay for.

headline_suffix: — only working sets and heavy warm-ups belong on the tally.
why_it_matters: If you're logging 'chest = 18 sets/week' but six of those are empty-bar bench, your real number is 12. Track honestly. The dose-response curve only knows about the sets that actually challenged the tissue.

headline_prefix: A rehabbed movement is ready to come back only after
headline_suffix: plus a light session tolerated for two reps in a row.
why_it_matters: Don't reintroduce dips or deep flyes the first day they feel okay. Two weeks of no pain in rehab drills, full range restored, then one light session, then a second. Only then load it. Rushing this turns a 4-week rehab into a 4-month one.
